name: Brief explanation request
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person asked Claude to explain a tax reporting notice from Revolut Securities Europe UAB regarding 2025 investment activity. Claude summarized that Revolut had reported gross proceeds from sales and withdrawals to Lithuanian tax authorities (VMI) under various income categories, including category 12 for financial instrument transfers, category 26A for dividends, and category 67 for interest. Claude noted that Revolut did not report acquisition costs or fees, and advised the person to manually add these when filing their GPM311 annual income declaration to avoid being taxed on the full proceeds rather than actual profit.

The person then asked a clarifying question — whether Revolut had reported only withdrawal amounts but not purchase amounts — and Claude confirmed this was correct, explaining that the pre-filled VMI declaration would reflect only the sell side, leaving the person responsible for entering their cost basis. Claude suggested the Revolut app's P&L statements as a source for cost basis information. The person responded informally ("bs :)"), and Claude acknowledged the frustration in a casual, agreeable tone. The conversation was brief and informal, with the person communicating concisely and Claude matching that register.

content.text: Explain me breefly

attachments.extracted_content:
Hi Petras,

This is a reminder about your investments with Revolut Securities Europe UAB (RSEUAB) and the tax information shared with your local tax authorities for 2025.

Read on to understand more about your statements for your RSEUAB investment accounts (including Flexible Cash Funds) and the tax info we've shared.

Understanding your Flexible Cash Funds statements
When you withdraw from Flexible Cash Funds, Revolut Securities Europe UAB sells your shares in the corresponding money market fund on your behalf. This appears in your statement as “Total value sold.”
The value of your purchased shares remained unchanged throughout 2025. As a result, the “Total value sold” matches the original purchase price of your shares in the corresponding money market fund.
“Total earned interest” reflects the returns you’ve made from holding money market fund shares.
“Total fees” represents the cost of this investment service and is deducted daily from your gross interest.
For more details on your investments through Flexible Cash Funds, check the Revolut app.

How to access your Flexible Cash Funds statements
In the Revolut app, tap your profile icon on the top left corner of your home screen
Go to Documents & Statements → Savings & Funds
Choose the relevant period
You can select multiple Flexible Cash Funds to generate a consolidated statement, grouping them by currency (for example, by money market fund).

Flexible Cash Funds information we've given to tax authorities
As required by law, we’ve reported the combined “Total value sold” for all your Flexible Cash Funds in 2025 to your local tax authority as “Income from title transfer of financial instruments, income received from marketing of derivatives not subject to tax relief (12).”
For sales in USD or GBP money market funds, amounts were converted to EUR using the applicable closing exchange rate on the date of each withdrawal.
Additionally, we reported the combined “Total earned interest” (before deducting any fees) as “Interest on non-equity securities (67)”, converting any GBP or USD interest to EUR using the applicable exchange rate on the date it was earned.
Please note that we are not required to and did not report:

Fee information
Amount of acquisition and selling expenses
Please ensure you understand and declare the necessary details for your investments made through RSEUAB.

Depending on your personal circumstances, you may be able to adjust your personal income declaration and change the amounts reported under “Income from title transfer of financial instruments, income received from marketing of derivatives not subject to tax relief (12)” to “Income from title transfer of financial instruments, income received from marketing of derivatives subject to tax relief (11).”

Understanding Brokerage, Robo-Advisor, and CFDs account statements
If you had a Brokerage account in 2025, you could've invested in US and EU stocks (including EU ETFs), as well as corporate or government bonds. If you had a Robo-Advisor account in 2025, your investments would have been in selected EU ETFs managed by RSEUAB. In addition, eligible customers could have a CFDs account, allowing them to trade Contracts for Difference (CFDs) across a range of underlying asset classes.

You can find details of these investments in the 2025 Profits and Losses (P&L) statements for the relevant account, including:

Financial instruments sold: listed under “Sells” section, includes “Gross proceeds” from sell orders, corresponding “Cost basis” (purchase price), and any incurred fees
Bond redemptions (for example, principal repayments): listed under the “Sells” section, shows “Gross proceeds” (principal redemption price) and “Cost basis” (principal purchase price)
Income from dividends and coupons: any dividends or coupon payments received during the period appear under “Other income & fees”
How to generate your Brokerage, Robo-Advisor, and CFDs account P&L statements
In the Revolut app, go to Invest in the bottom menu
Tap More (…) → Documents
Select Brokerage account, Robo-Advisor or CFDs and tap Profit & Loss statement
Choose the relevant period
If your investment account is closed, you won't be able to generate statements in the app. In that case, contact us via the in-app chat and we’ll generate the statements for your closed accounts.

Brokerage, Robo-Advisor, and CFDs account information we've given to tax authorities
As required by law, we’ve reported the total amount from all sold financial instruments in your Brokerage, Robo-Advisor and CFDs accounts in 2025 as “Income from title transfer of financial instruments, income received from marketing of derivatives not subject to tax relief (12).” This is shown in your P&L statements under “Sells” as “Gross proceeds” for Brokerage and Robo-Advisor accounts and under “Close trades” as “Gross PnL” for CFDs. Please note that for transactions in USD, amounts were converted to EUR using the applicable closing exchange rate.
If applicable, we’ve reported dividends from stocks and ETFs as “Dividends (26A)”, found in the “Other income & fees” section of your P&L statements under “Gross amount.” Any withholding tax on dividends is also reported in the same section as “Withholding tax.”
We’ve reported bond coupon payments as “Interest on non-equity securities (67)”, found in the “Other income & fees” section of your P&L statements under “Gross amount”. Any withholding tax on these payments is also reported in the same section as “Withholding tax.”
If a bond redemption resulted in a higher redemption price than the original acquisition price, we’ve reported the difference as “Interest on non-equity securities (67)”, found in the “Sells” section of your Brokerage account P&L statement as “Gross P&L”.
Please ensure you understand and declare the necessary details for your investments made through RSEUAB.

Depending on your personal circumstances, you may be able to adjust your personal income declaration and change the amounts reported under “Income from title transfer of financial instruments, income received from marketing of derivatives not subject to tax relief (12)” to “Income from title transfer of financial instruments, income received from marketing of derivatives subject to tax relief (11).”

Additional tax information
Revolut Securities Europe UAB does not provide any tax, legal, or accounting advice. Information in this email should not be relied on for tax, legal, or accounting advice.

It’s your responsibility to understand how to declare your income, and to make any applicable tax payments. You should provide your relevant tax authorities with all necessary information in relation to any instrument you purchase via our investment platform.

This is a tax reporting notice from Revolut Securities Europe UAB about your 2025 investments. Here's the gist:

**Flexible Cash Funds** — When you withdrew money, Revolut sold your money market fund shares. They reported the total sold value to Lithuanian tax authorities as income under category 12 (title transfer of financial instruments), and your earned interest under category 67 (interest on non-equity securities). **Brokerage / Robo-Advisor / CFDs** — Similarly, gross proceeds from any sold stocks, ETFs, bonds, or CFDs were reported under category 12. Dividends were reported under 26A, and bond coupons under 67. Withholding tax info was also shared.

Revolut explicitly says they're not giving tax advice, so if anything looks unclear in your declaration, it's worth double-checking with VMI guidance or a tax advisor.

chat_messages.text:
Exactly. Revolut reported only the **sell side** — gross proceeds from withdrawals/sales — to VMI. They did **not** report:

- How much you originally paid (acquisition/purchase price)
- Any fees you incurred

So from VMI's perspective, the pre-filled declaration will look like all the reported amount is pure income, which it isn't. You need to manually add your cost basis (what you paid to buy those shares/funds) and any relevant fees when filing your declaration, otherwise you'd be taxed on the full amount instead of just the actual profit.

You can find the acquisition costs in your P&L statements in the Revolut app — they're listed as "Cost basis" under the Sells section.

name: Managing context for multi-session feature implementation
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The user sought advice on context management strategy for a Claude Code project where they had developed a detailed 2-session implementation plan for a new feature but had already consumed 60% of their available context. They presented three options: clearing context and starting fresh across 2 sessions, compacting the current context before proceeding to stage 1, or continuing with the current situation since it contained highly relevant context.

Claude recommended option 1 (clearing context and starting fresh) based on the principle that planning context differs significantly from implementation context. The reasoning centered on implementation generating substantial new context through file operations, code generation, and debugging, making the 60% usage before writing any code a significant risk for hitting context limits mid-implementation. Claude emphasized that the detailed plan itself serves as the key artifact rather than the conversational context that created it.

The recommended workflow involved saving the implementation plan to a project file (such as `docs/implementation-plan.md` or `CLAUDE.md`), then starting fresh sessions that reference the written plan for each implementation stage. This approach was favored over context compaction due to the lossy nature of compaction, which might drop important nuances or decisions from the planning phase, whereas an explicit written plan provides better control over what information carries forward to the implementation phases.

chat_messages.text:
in claude code i made well thought 2 session implementation plan for new feature in a project. and i have used 60 % of context, what is the best option to proceed.
Clear context and start new sesion and execute a plan i 2 sesions
Compact the current context(was making the detailed plan) and then start on stage1
Go with current situation and start implementing as it is the best relavent context 
or..?

content.text:
in claude code i made well thought 2 session implementation plan for new feature in a project. and i have used 60 % of context, what is the best option to proceed.
Clear context and start new sesion and execute a plan i 2 sesions
Compact the current context(was making the detailed plan) and then start on stage1
Go with current situation and start implementing as it is the best relavent context 
or..?

chat_messages.text:
Great question, Petras. This is a common dilemma with Claude Code context management.

**Go with option 1: Clear context and start fresh across 2 sessions.** Here's why:

**Implementation is different from planning.** The planning session built up a lot of "thinking" context — exploration, alternatives considered, back-and-forth reasoning. That context is useful for *making* the plan but mostly dead weight during *execution*. You'd be burning tokens on context that doesn't help the actual coding.

**60% used before writing a single line of code is risky.** Implementation generates a lot of context fast — file reads, code generation, error fixing, test runs. You'd likely hit the limit mid-way through stage 1 and be forced into a compaction or new session at an awkward point.

**The plan is your artifact — not the context.** As long as your 2-stage plan is written down (in a markdown file, CLAUDE.md, or similar), a fresh session can pick it up and execute cleanly with full context budget available.

**My recommended workflow:**

1. Save the plan to a file in your project (e.g., `docs/implementation-plan.md` or even in `CLAUDE.md`)
2. Start fresh session → "Read the implementation plan in X, execute stage 1"
3. New session → "Read the plan, stage 1 is complete, execute stage 2"

**Why not compaction?** It *can* work, but compaction is lossy — it might drop nuances from your plan or lose relevant decisions you made. Starting clean with an explicit written plan gives you more control over what carries forward.

name: Extracting unique emails from delimited fields
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person needed to extract a deduplicated list of email addresses from an uploaded Excel file called `Active_Stations_3-9-2026_1-44-17_PM.xlsx`. They noted that some fields contained multiple email values separated by commas or semicolons, and the goal was to produce a clean list of unique mailboxes. Claude identified three relevant email columns in the file — `TDS_Email`, `TP_Emails`, and `Planner_Email` — then parsed all values by splitting on both `,` and `;` delimiters, stripped whitespace, lowercased all addresses, and deduplicated the results. The output was 24 unique emails saved to a formatted Excel file (`unique_emails.xlsx`) with a styled header and auto-sized column width.

**Tool Knowledge**

For Excel processing in this environment, Python with `pandas` and `openpyxl` was used directly via bash. The pattern `re.split(r'[,;]+', val)` reliably handled multi-delimiter splitting in a single pass. Reading the file used `pd.read_excel('/mnt/user-data/uploads/[filename]')` and output was saved to `/mnt/user-data/outputs/`. Iterating over specific named columns rather than scanning all columns was the approach once email columns were identified by checking for `@` in sample values.

input.description: Check xlsx skill

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: xlsx
     3	description: "Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like \"the xlsx in my downloads\") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# Requirements for Outputs
     8	
     9	## All Excel files
    10	
    11	### Professional Font
    12	- Use a consistent, professional font (e.g., Arial, Times New Roman) for all deliverables unless otherwise instructed by the user
    13	
    14	### Zero Formula Errors
    15	- Every Excel model MUST be delivered with ZERO formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #N/A, #NAME?)
    16	
    17	### Preserve Existing Templates (when updating templates)
    18	- Study and EXACTLY match existing format, style, and conventions when modifying files
    19	- Never impose standardized formatting on files with established patterns
    20	- Existing template conventions ALWAYS override these guidelines
    21	
    22	## Financial models
    23	
    24	### Color Coding Standards
    25	Unless otherwise stated by the user or existing template
    26	
    27	#### Industry-Standard Color Conventions
    28	- **Blue text (RGB: 0,0,255)**: Hardcoded inputs, and numbers users will change for scenarios
    29	- **Black text (RGB: 0,0,0)**: ALL formulas and calculations
    30	- **Green text (RGB: 0,128,0)**: Links pulling from other worksheets within same workbook
    31	- **Red text (RGB: 255,0,0)**: External links to other files
    32	- **Yellow background (RGB: 255,255,0)**: Key assumptions needing attention or cells that need to be updated
    33	
    34	### Number Formatting Standards
    35	
    36	#### Required Format Rules
    37	- **Years**: Format as text strings (e.g., "2024" not "2,024")
    38	- **Currency**: Use $#,##0 format; ALWAYS specify units in headers ("Revenue ($mm)")
    39	- **Zeros**: Use number formatting to make all zeros "-", including percentages (e.g., "$#,##0;($#,##0);-")
    40	- **Percentages**: Default to 0.0% format (one decimal)
    41	- **Multiples**: Format as 0.0x for valuation multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/E)
    42	- **Negative numbers**: Use parentheses (123) not minus -123
    43	
    44	### Formula Construction Rules
    45	
    46	#### Assumptions Placement
    47	- Place ALL assumptions (growth rates, margins, multiples, etc.) in separate assumption cells
    48	- Use cell references instead of hardcoded values in formulas
    49	- Example: Use =B5*(1+$B$6) instead of =B5*1.05
    50	
    51	#### Formula Error Prevention
    52	- Verify all cell references are correct
    53	- Check for off-by-one errors in ranges
    54	- Ensure consistent formulas across all projection periods
    55	- Test with edge cases (zero values, negative numbers)
    56	- Verify no unintended circular references
    57	
    58	#### Documentation Requirements for Hardcodes
    59	- Comment or in cells beside (if end of table). Format: "Source: [System/Document], [Date], [Specific Reference], [URL if applicable]"
    60	- Examples:
    61	  - "Source: Company 10-K, FY2024, Page 45, Revenue Note, [SEC EDGAR URL]"
    62	  - "Source: Company 10-Q, Q2 2025, Exhibit 99.1, [SEC EDGAR URL]"
    63	  - "Source: Bloomberg Terminal, 8/15/2025, AAPL US Equity"
    64	  - "Source: FactSet, 8/20/2025, Consensus Estimates Screen"
    65	
    66	# XLSX creation, editing, and analysis
    67	
    68	## Overview
    69	
    70	A user may ask you to create, edit, or analyze the contents of an .xlsx file. You have different tools and workflows available for different tasks.
    71	
    72	## Important Requirements
    73	
    74	**LibreOffice Required for Formula Recalculation**: You can assume LibreOffice is installed for recalculating formula values using the `scripts/recalc.py` script. The script automatically configures LibreOffice on first run, including in sandboxed environments where Unix sockets are restricted (handled by `scripts/office/soffice.py`)
    75	
    76	## Reading and analyzing data
    77	
    78	### Data analysis with pandas
    79	For data analysis, visualization, and basic operations, use **pandas** which provides powerful data manipulation capabilities:
    80	
    81	```python
    82	import pandas as pd
    83	
    84	# Read Excel
    85	df = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx')  # Default: first sheet
    86	all_sheets = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', sheet_name=None)  # All sheets as dict
    87	
    88	# Analyze
    89	df.head()      # Preview data
    90	df.info()      # Column info
    91	df.describe()  # Statistics
    92	
    93	# Write Excel
    94	df.to_excel('output.xlsx', index=False)
    95	```
    96	
    97	## Excel File Workflows
    98	
    99	## CRITICAL: Use Formulas, Not Hardcoded Values
   100	
   101	**Always use Excel formulas instead of calculating values in Python and hardcoding them.** This ensures the spreadsheet remains dynamic and updateable.
   102	
   103	### ❌ WRONG - Hardcoding Calculated Values
   104	```python
   105	# Bad: Calculating in Python and hardcoding result
   106	total = df['Sales'].sum()
   107	sheet['B10'] = total  # Hardcodes 5000
   108	
   109	# Bad: Computing growth rate in Python
   110	growth = (df.iloc[-1]['Revenue'] - df.iloc[0]['Revenue']) / df.iloc[0]['Revenue']
   111	sheet['C5'] = growth  # Hardcodes 0.15
   112	
   113	# Bad: Python calculation for average
   114	avg = sum(values) / len(values)
   115	sheet['D20'] = avg  # Hardcodes 42.5
   116	```
   117	
   118	### ✅ CORRECT - Using Excel Formulas
   119	```python
   120	# Good: Let Excel calculate the sum
   121	sheet['B10'] = '=SUM(B2:B9)'
   122	
   123	# Good: Growth rate as Excel formula
   124	sheet['C5'] = '=(C4-C2)/C2'
   125	
   126	# Good: Average using Excel function
   127	sheet['D20'] = '=AVERAGE(D2:D19)'
   128	```
   129	
   130	This applies to ALL calculations - totals, percentages, ratios, differences, etc. The spreadsheet should be able to recalculate when source data changes.
   131	
   132	## Common Workflow
   133	1. **Choose tool**: pandas for data, openpyxl for formulas/formatting
   134	2. **Create/Load**: Create new workbook or load existing file
   135	3. **Modify**: Add/edit data, formulas, and formatting
   136	4. **Save**: Write to file
   137	5. **Recalculate formulas (MANDATORY IF USING FORMULAS)**: Use the scripts/recalc.py script
   138	   ```bash
   139	   python scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx
   140	   ```
   141	6. **Verify and fix any errors**: 
   142	   - The script returns JSON with error details
   143	   - If `status` is `errors_found`, check `error_summary` for specific error types and locations
   144	   - Fix the identified errors and recalculate again
   145	   - Common errors to fix:
   146	     - `#REF!`: Invalid cell references
   147	     - `#DIV/0!`: Division by zero
   148	     - `#VALUE!`: Wrong data type in formula
   149	     - `#NAME?`: Unrecognized formula name
   150	
   151	### Creating new Excel files
   152	
   153	```python
   154	# Using openpyxl for formulas and formatting
   155	from openpyxl import Workbook
   156	from openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment
   157	
   158	wb = Workbook()
   159	sheet = wb.active
   160	
   161	# Add data
   162	sheet['A1'] = 'Hello'
   163	sheet['B1'] = 'World'
   164	sheet.append(['Row', 'of', 'data'])
   165	
   166	# Add formula
   167	sheet['B2'] = '=SUM(A1:A10)'
   168	
   169	# Formatting
   170	sheet['A1'].font = Font(bold=True, color='FF0000')
   171	sheet['A1'].fill = PatternFill('solid', start_color='FFFF00')
   172	sheet['A1'].alignment = Alignment(horizontal='center')
   173	
   174	# Column width
   175	sheet.column_dimensions['A'].width = 20
   176	
   177	wb.save('output.xlsx')
   178	```
   179	
   180	### Editing existing Excel files
   181	
   182	```python
   183	# Using openpyxl to preserve formulas and formatting
   184	from openpyxl import load_workbook
   185	
   186	# Load existing file
   187	wb = load_workbook('existing.xlsx')
   188	sheet = wb.active  # or wb['SheetName'] for specific sheet
   189	
   190	# Working with multiple sheets
   191	for sheet_name in wb.sheetnames:
   192	    sheet = wb[sheet_name]
   193	    print(f"Sheet: {sheet_name}")
   194	
   195	# Modify cells
   196	sheet['A1'] = 'New Value'
   197	sheet.insert_rows(2)  # Insert row at position 2
   198	sheet.delete_cols(3)  # Delete column 3
   199	
   200	# Add new sheet
   201	new_sheet = wb.create_sheet('NewSheet')
   202	new_sheet['A1'] = 'Data'
   203	
   204	wb.save('modified.xlsx')
   205	```
   206	
   207	## Recalculating formulas
   208	
   209	Excel files created or modified by openpyxl contain formulas as strings but not calculated values. Use the provided `scripts/recalc.py` script to recalculate formulas:
   210	
   211	```bash
   212	python scripts/recalc.py <excel_file> [timeout_seconds]
   213	```
   214	
   215	Example:
   216	```bash
   217	python scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx 30
   218	```
   219	
   220	The script:
   221	- Automatically sets up LibreOffice macro on first run
   222	- Recalculates all formulas in all sheets
   223	- Scans ALL cells for Excel errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, etc.)
   224	- Returns JSON with detailed error locations and counts
   225	- Works on both Linux and macOS
   226	
   227	## Formula Verification Checklist
   228	
   229	Quick checks to ensure formulas work correctly:
   230	
   231	### Essential Verification
   232	- [ ] **Test 2-3 sample references**: Verify they pull correct values before building full model
   233	- [ ] **Column mapping**: Confirm Excel columns match (e.g., column 64 = BL, not BK)
   234	- [ ] **Row offset**: Remember Excel rows are 1-indexed (DataFrame row 5 = Excel row 6)
   235	
   236	### Common Pitfalls
   237	- [ ] **NaN handling**: Check for null values with `pd.notna()`
   238	- [ ] **Far-right columns**: FY data often in columns 50+ 
   239	- [ ] **Multiple matches**: Search all occurrences, not just first
   240	- [ ] **Division by zero**: Check denominators before using `/` in formulas (#DIV/0!)
   241	- [ ] **Wrong references**: Verify all cell references point to intended cells (#REF!)
   242	- [ ] **Cross-sheet references**: Use correct format (Sheet1!A1) for linking sheets
   243	
   244	### Formula Testing Strategy
   245	- [ ] **Start small**: Test formulas on 2-3 cells before applying broadly
   246	- [ ] **Verify dependencies**: Check all cells referenced in formulas exist
   247	- [ ] **Test edge cases**: Include zero, negative, and very large values
   248	
   249	### Interpreting scripts/recalc.py Output
   250	The script returns JSON with error details:
   251	```json
   252	{
   253	  "status": "success",           // or "errors_found"
   254	  "total_errors": 0,              // Total error count
   255	  "total_formulas": 42,           // Number of formulas in file
   256	  "error_summary": {              // Only present if errors found
   257	    "#REF!": {
   258	      "count": 2,
   259	      "locations": ["Sheet1!B5", "Sheet1!C10"]
   260	    }
   261	  }
   262	}
   263	```
   264	
   265	## Best Practices
   266	
   267	### Library Selection
   268	- **pandas**: Best for data analysis, bulk operations, and simple data export
   269	- **openpyxl**: Best for complex formatting, formulas, and Excel-specific features
   270	
   271	### Working with openpyxl
   272	- Cell indices are 1-based (row=1, column=1 refers to cell A1)
   273	- Use `data_only=True` to read calculated values: `load_workbook('file.xlsx', data_only=True)`
   274	- **Warning**: If opened with `data_only=True` and saved, formulas are replaced with values and permanently lost
   275	- For large files: Use `read_only=True` for reading or `write_only=True` for writing
   276	- Formulas are preserved but not evaluated - use scripts/recalc.py to update values
   277	
   278	### Working with pandas
   279	- Specify data types to avoid inference issues: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', dtype={'id': str})`
   280	- For large files, read specific columns: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', usecols=['A', 'C', 'E'])`
   281	- Handle dates properly: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', parse_dates=['date_column'])`
   282	
   283	## Code Style Guidelines
   284	**IMPORTANT**: When generating Python code for Excel operations:
   285	- Write minimal, concise Python code without unnecessary comments
   286	- Avoid verbose variable names and redundant operations
   287	- Avoid unnecessary print statements
   288	
   289	**For Excel files themselves**:
   290	- Add comments to cells with complex formulas or important assumptions
   291	- Document data sources for hardcoded values
   292	- Include notes for key calculations and model sections

input.command:
cd /home/claude && python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
import re
from openpyxl import Workbook
from openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment

df = pd.read_excel('/mnt/user-data/uploads/Active_Stations_3-9-2026_1-44-17_PM.xlsx')

emails = set()
for col in ['TDS_Email', 'TP_Emails', 'Planner_Email']:
    for val in df[col].dropna().astype(str):
        for p in re.split(r'[,;]+', val):
            p = p.strip().lower()
            if '@' in p:
                emails.add(p)

sorted_emails = sorted(emails)

wb = Workbook()
ws = wb.active
ws.title = "Unique Emails"

# Header
ws['A1'] = 'Email'
ws['A1'].font = Font(bold=True, name='Arial')
ws['A1'].fill = PatternFill('solid', start_color='4472C4')
ws['A1'].font = Font(bold=True, name='Arial', color='FFFFFF')

for i, email in enumerate(sorted_emails, start=2):
    ws.cell(row=i, column=1, value=email).font = Font(name='Arial')

ws.column_dimensions['A'].width = 45

out = '/mnt/user-data/outputs/unique_emails.xlsx'
wb.save(out)
print(f"Saved {len(sorted_emails)} unique emails")
EOF

attachments.extracted_content:
# Open Brain --- AI-Native Organizational Memory

**Document Type:** System Product Specification (SPEC)\
**Version:** v0.1\
**Status:** Draft\
**Date:** 2026

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 1. Problem Statement

Modern organizations lose large amounts of contextual knowledge because
information is fragmented across tools such as:

-   Slack discussions
-   Meeting notes
-   Documents
-   Emails
-   AI chat sessions

Key decisions, reasoning, and relationships between ideas are rarely
preserved in a **machine-readable form**.

Traditional note systems are designed for **humans to read**, not for
**AI systems to reason over**.

Open Brain proposes a **shared AI-readable knowledge infrastructure**
that preserves organizational intelligence.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 2. Goals

### G1 --- Zero-friction capture

Users must be able to store information with minimal effort.

### G2 --- Structured AI-readable memory

Knowledge must be normalized into structured entities.

### G3 --- Reliable retrieval

AI systems must retrieve relevant context with high precision.

### G4 --- Durable knowledge

Decisions and reasoning must remain accessible long-term.

### G5 --- Tool independence

Knowledge must live in an open database independent of SaaS vendors.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 3. Non‑Goals (MVP)

The MVP will NOT include:

-   Autonomous AI agents writing memory without review
-   Complex enterprise permission models
-   Real-time collaborative editing
-   Advanced knowledge graph inference
-   Distributed infrastructure

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 4. Design Principles

### P1 --- Raw data is never lost

All ingested data is stored in its original form.

### P2 --- Memory is append-only

Knowledge entries are never deleted or overwritten.

### P3 --- AI assists but does not own memory

AI can propose structure but humans remain authoritative.

### P4 --- Hybrid retrieval

Retrieval must combine:

-   semantic similarity
-   keyword search
-   structured filters
-   recency weighting

### P5 --- Operational simplicity

PostgreSQL is the primary database.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 5. High-Level Architecture

Input Layer\
↓\
Raw Memory Store\
↓\
Refinement Pipeline\
↓\
Structured Memory Layer\
↓\
Retrieval Engine\
↓\
AI Interface

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 6. Input Layer

Initial supported sources:

-   Slack channel
-   REST API
-   CLI tool

Future sources:

-   Voice capture
-   Browser extension
-   Email ingestion

Each input event creates a **Raw Memory Entry**.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 7. Raw Memory Store

  Field        Type        Description
  ------------ ----------- -------------------
  id           uuid        unique identifier
  source       text        ingestion source
  raw_text     text        original content
  author       text        user id
  created_at   timestamp   ingestion time
  metadata     jsonb       optional metadata

  : `raw_memory`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 8. Refinement Pipeline

Pipeline stages:

Raw Input\
↓\
Text Normalization\
↓\
Structured Extraction\
↓\
Validation\
↓\
Embedding Generation\
↓\
Structured Memory Write

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 8.1 Text Normalization

-   Removes transcription artifacts
-   Corrects typos
-   Splits large entries

Output:

`normalized_text`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 8.2 Structured Extraction

Expected JSON schema:

``` json
{
  "summary": "string",
  "type": "note | task | decision | meeting | idea",
  "entities": [
    { "name": "string", "type": "person|project|concept" }
  ],
  "tasks": [
    {
      "description": "string",
      "owner": "string",
      "due_date": "timestamp"
    }
  ],
  "decisions": [
    {
      "decision": "string",
      "reasoning": "string",
      "alternatives": ["string"]
    }
  ]
}
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 8.3 Validation Layer

Validation checks:

-   Schema correctness
-   Required fields
-   Entity normalization

Invalid outputs are reprocessed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 8.4 Embedding Generation

Embeddings generated from normalized text.

Stored as:

`vector(1536)`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 9. Structured Memory Layer

Database: PostgreSQL + pgvector

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 9.1 Memory Items

  Field              Type
  ------------------ -----------
  id                 uuid
  raw_id             uuid
  type               text
  content            text
  summary            text
  importance_score   float
  embedding          vector
  created_at         timestamp

  : `memory_items`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 9.2 Entities

  Field        Type
  ------------ -----------
  id           uuid
  name         text
  type         text
  created_at   timestamp

  : `entities`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 9.3 Entity Relations

  Field           Type
  --------------- ------
  from_entity     uuid
  to_entity       uuid
  relation_type   text

  : `entity_relations`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 9.4 Tasks

  Field         Type
  ------------- -----------
  id            uuid
  description   text
  owner         text
  due_date      timestamp
  status        text

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 9.5 Decisions

  Field          Type
  -------------- -------
  id             uuid
  memory_id      uuid
  decision       text
  reasoning      text
  alternatives   jsonb

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 10. Retrieval Engine

Hybrid retrieval process:

User Query\
↓\
Query Embedding\
↓\
Vector Search\
↓\
Keyword Search\
↓\
Structured Filtering\
↓\
Ranking

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Ranking Formula

score =\
semantic_similarity\
+ keyword_match\
+ recency_weight\
+ importance_score

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 11. Importance Scoring

Importance signals:

-   references from other entries
-   contains decisions
-   contains tasks
-   retrieval frequency

Range:

0.0 = trivial note\
1.0 = critical decision

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 12. Weekly Synthesis

A scheduled job generates a **Weekly Brain Report** summarizing:

-   new entities
-   major decisions
-   open tasks
-   emerging topics
-   project relationships

The report is stored as a memory item.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 13. AI Interface

### REST API

POST `/memory`\
GET `/search`\
GET `/entity/{id}`

### MCP Adapter (future)

Allows AI tools to query the brain through Model Context Protocol.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 14. Security Model

Roles:

User\
System\
AI Agent

AI agents can:

-   read memory

AI agents cannot:

-   delete entries
-   modify decisions
-   change tasks

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 15. Observability

System metrics:

-   ingestion rate
-   refinement errors
-   retrieval latency
-   embedding failures

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 16. Success Metrics

-   Retrieval accuracy
-   Decision recall success
-   Knowledge capture rate
-   Time saved searching

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 17. Implementation Roadmap

## Phase 1 --- Core Memory (2 weeks)

-   Database schema
-   Ingestion API
-   Raw memory storage
-   Refinement pipeline
-   Embeddings
-   Hybrid search

## Phase 2 --- Slack Integration (2--4 weeks)

-   Slack ingestion bot
-   Entity extraction
-   Task extraction
-   Decision extraction

## Phase 3 --- Weekly Synthesis (1 month)

-   Knowledge reports
-   Relationship analysis
-   Importance scoring

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 18. Risks

### Data Swamp

Low quality refinement may degrade knowledge quality.

Mitigation: strict schema + validation.

### Retrieval Noise

Vector search may produce irrelevant results.

Mitigation: hybrid ranking.

### AI Hallucination

LLM extraction errors.

Mitigation: validation and human review for decisions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 19. Future Extensions

-   Full knowledge graph reasoning
-   Agent memory write proposals
-   Automated retrospectives
-   Personal vs team memory partitions
-   Cross‑organization knowledge graphs

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**End of Specification**

chat_messages.text:
I've read through the Open Brain spec carefully. It's a solid foundation — the core idea of making organizational knowledge AI-readable rather than just human-readable is strong, and the PostgreSQL + pgvector choice keeps things operationally simple.

Here are the questions and concerns that came up:

**Architecture & scope**

- The spec describes this as organizational/team infrastructure, but your current setup (VPS, Cadence project) suggests you'd be the primary user initially. Are you building this for a team from day one, or starting as a single-user system that could grow? This significantly affects auth, multi-tenancy, and how much of the security model matters in Phase 1.
- How does this relate to Cadence? Is Open Brain the "second brain" layer that Cadence would consume, or are these separate projects?

**Refinement pipeline**

- The pipeline assumes LLM-powered extraction (structured extraction, normalization, embedding). Which LLM are you planning to use for the refinement steps? Local model on VPS, or API calls to Claude/OpenAI? This affects cost, latency, and offline capability.
- "Invalid outputs are reprocessed" — this needs a circuit breaker. What happens after N failures? Silent drop? Dead letter queue? Human review flag?

**Data model concerns**

- "Memory is append-only" (P2) is a strong principle but creates practical issues. What about corrections? If a decision is recorded incorrectly, how do you fix it without violating append-only? Superseding entries with back-references?
- The `importance_score` is described as a signal-based float, but there's no mention of when/how it gets recalculated. Is it set once at ingestion, or updated as retrieval frequency changes?
- Entity normalization could be tricky — "Petras", "P.", "me" might all refer to the same person. What's the dedup/merge strategy?

**Retrieval**

- The ranking formula lists four terms but no weights. Are these learned, manually tuned, or equal? This will heavily affect retrieval quality.
- How large do you expect the memory store to get in 6 months? This affects whether naive pgvector search is sufficient or you'll need indexing strategies (IVFFlat, HNSW) from the start.

**Practical gaps**

- No mention of the embedding model. OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small`? Something local? This is a critical dependency.
- The Weekly Synthesis job — what generates it? Another LLM call summarizing recent memory items?
- No mention of how the CLI tool works for input. Is it a quick `ob add "decided to use Rust for the parser"` style command, or something more structured?

These aren't blockers, but clarifying them will make the implementation plan much tighter. Go ahead with your wishlist and we can factor everything in together.

chat_messages.text:
The Cadense project it was a good try, but for now forget about it. The main goal is firstly to build open brain - second brain- thoughts lake all of above. Human and AI readable.

1. Building for my own use only
2. thinking about Cloud LLM like claude-3.5-haiku or OpenAI GPT-4.1-mini
3. Refinement Failure Handling
You are absolutely right — “reprocess invalid outputs” needs clear failure handling.
Revised design:

```
attempt 1 → extraction
attempt 2 → retry with stricter prompt
attempt 3 → retry with repair prompt
```

If still invalid:

```
→ Dead Letter Queue
```

Dead letter table:

```
failed_refinements
```

Schema:

```
id
raw_id
error_reason
attempt_count
last_output
created_at
```

Possible handling:

```
manual review
or
periodic reprocessing
```

4.Superseding entries.
Example:

```
Memory A
"Decision: use MongoDB"
```

Later corrected:

```
Memory B
type: correction
supersedes: A
content: "Decision changed to PostgreSQL"
```

Retrieval rule:

```
latest version wins
```

Implementation:

```
memory_items
  supersedes_memory_id
```

So the chain becomes:

```
A → B
```

This keeps:
* audit history
* data integrity
5.The spec currently treats it as static, but that’s insufficient.
Importance should evolve over time.
We split it into two signals:

```
base_importance
dynamic_importance
```

Base importance (set during ingestion)
Signals:

```
contains decision
contains task
long entry
```

Dynamic importance (recalculated daily)
Signals:

```
retrieval frequency
references by other entries
entity centrality
```

Final score:

```
importance_score =
0.6 * base
+ 0.4 * dynamic
```

Recalculation job:

```
daily importance update
```

6.Entity Normalization Problem
This is one of the hardest problems in knowledge systems.
Example:

```
"Petras"
"P."
"me"
```

Possible approaches:
Stage 1 — naive normalization
Lowercase + trimming:

```
petras
p.
me
```

Not enough.
Stage 2 — fuzzy matching
Use similarity threshold.
Example:

```
petras vs petras k
```

If similarity > 0.9 → merge candidate.
Stage 3 — human confirmation
Possible table:

```
entity_aliases
```

Example:

```
alias: "me"
entity_id: petras
```

Later improvement:
Entity resolution model.
But MVP should stay simple.

7.The spec currently shows:

```
score =
semantic_similarity
+ keyword_match
+ recency
+ importance
```

But weights matter.
Initial heuristic:

```
0.5 semantic similarity
0.2 keyword match
0.2 importance
0.1 recency
```

Why?
Semantic relevance is the primary signal.
Recency should not dominate.
These weights should eventually be learned, but manual tuning is fine initially.

8.Expected Data Size
Let’s estimate realistically.
Assume heavy usage:

```
50 entries/day
```

In 6 months:

```
≈ 9000 entries
```

Vector size:

```
1536 dimensions
≈ 6 KB per vector
```

Storage:

```
9000 * 6 KB ≈ 54 MB
```

Very small.
Conclusion
For at least 1–2 years, simple pgvector search is enough.
Use:

```
HNSW index
```

Example SQL:

```
CREATE INDEX memory_embedding_idx
ON memory_items
USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops);
```

9.Embedding Model Choice
This must be explicitly defined.
Best current option:
OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small`
Reasons:

```
cheap
good quality
1536 dimensions
```

Cost:

```
~$0.02 per 1M tokens
```

Negligible.
9. Weekly Synthesis Generator
Yes — this is another LLM job.
Process:

```
Fetch last week's memory
↓
cluster by topic
↓
extract major decisions
↓
summarize
```

Prompt example:

```
Summarize the week's knowledge:

- key decisions
- emerging themes
- new entities
- unresolved tasks
```

Output stored as:

```
memory_items
type = weekly_report
```

10. CLI Tool Design
Good catch — the spec was vague.
CLI should be extremely lightweight.
Example usage:

```
ob add "Decided to use Rust for the parser"
```

More structured:

```
ob task "Write ingestion API" --owner petras --due 2026-03-20
```

Decision entry:

```
ob decision "Use pgvector instead of Pinecone"
```

Under the hood:

```
CLI
↓
POST /memory
↓
refinement pipeline
```

The CLI is not responsible for structuring data.
LLM handles it.
11. One Design Change I Would Make
Right now the pipeline is synchronous.
Better:

```
ingest → queue → refine → store
```

Architecture:

```
API
↓
message queue
↓
worker
↓
database
```

Benefits:

```
non-blocking ingestion
retry logic
failure isolation
```

Queue options:
* Redis
* RabbitMQ
* Postgres queue
For MVP: Postgres queue is enough.
12. Most Important Missing Component
There is one thing still missing in the spec:
Context window builder
When an AI queries memory:

```
search results → ranked
↓
context builder
↓
LLM answer
```

This decides:

```
how many memories
which types
how summarized
```

Without this layer retrieval quality suffers.

The CLI is not responsible for structuring data.
LLM handles it.
11. One Design Change I Would Make
Right now the pipeline is synchronous.
Better:

```
ingest → queue → refine → store
```

Architecture:

```
API
↓
message queue
↓
worker
↓
database
```

Benefits:

```
non-blocking ingestion
retry logic
failure isolation
```

Queue options:
* Redis
* RabbitMQ
* Postgres queue
For MVP: Postgres queue is enough.
12. Most Important Missing Component
There is one thing still missing in the spec:
Context window builder
When an AI queries memory:

```
search results → ranked
↓
context builder
↓
LLM answer
```

This decides:

```
how many memories
which types
how summarized
```

Without this layer retrieval quality suffers.

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: docx
     3	description: "Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# DOCX creation, editing, and analysis
     8	
     9	## Overview
    10	
    11	A .docx file is a ZIP archive containing XML files.
    12	
    13	## Quick Reference
    14	
    15	| Task | Approach |
    16	|------|----------|
    17	| Read/analyze content | `pandoc` or unpack for raw XML |
    18	| Create new document | Use `docx-js` - see Creating New Documents below |
    19	| Edit existing document | Unpack → edit XML → repack - see Editing Existing Documents below |
    20	
    21	### Converting .doc to .docx
    22	
    23	Legacy `.doc` files must be converted before editing:
    24	
    25	```bash
    26	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to docx document.doc
    27	```
    28	
    29	### Reading Content
    30	
    31	```bash
    32	# Text extraction with tracked changes
    33	pandoc --track-changes=all document.docx -o output.md
    34	
    35	# Raw XML access
    36	python scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/
    37	```
    38	
    39	### Converting to Images
    40	
    41	```bash
    42	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to pdf document.docx
    43	pdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 document.pdf page
    44	```
    45	
    46	### Accepting Tracked Changes
    47	
    48	To produce a clean document with all tracked changes accepted (requires LibreOffice):
    49	
    50	```bash
    51	python scripts/accept_changes.py input.docx output.docx
    52	```
    53	
    54	---
    55	
    56	## Creating New Documents
    57	
    58	Generate .docx files with JavaScript, then validate. Install: `npm install -g docx`
    59	
    60	### Setup
    61	```javascript
    62	const { Document, Packer, Paragraph, TextRun, Table, TableRow, TableCell, ImageRun,
    63	        Header, Footer, AlignmentType, PageOrientation, LevelFormat, ExternalHyperlink,
    64	        InternalHyperlink, Bookmark, FootnoteReferenceRun, PositionalTab,
    65	        PositionalTabAlignment, PositionalTabRelativeTo, PositionalTabLeader,
    66	        TabStopType, TabStopPosition, Column, SectionType,
    67	        TableOfContents, HeadingLevel, BorderStyle, WidthType, ShadingType,
    68	        VerticalAlign, PageNumber, PageBreak } = require('docx');
    69	
    70	const doc = new Document({ sections: [{ children: [/* content */] }] });
    71	Packer.toBuffer(doc).then(buffer => fs.writeFileSync("doc.docx", buffer));
    72	```
    73	
    74	### Validation
    75	After creating the file, validate it. If validation fails, unpack, fix the XML, and repack.
    76	```bash
    77	python scripts/office/validate.py doc.docx
    78	```
    79	
    80	### Page Size
    81	
    82	```javascript
    83	// CRITICAL: docx-js defaults to A4, not US Letter
    84	// Always set page size explicitly for consistent results
    85	sections: [{
    86	  properties: {
    87	    page: {
    88	      size: {
    89	        width: 12240,   // 8.5 inches in DXA
    90	        height: 15840   // 11 inches in DXA
    91	      },
    92	      margin: { top: 1440, right: 1440, bottom: 1440, left: 1440 } // 1 inch margins
    93	    }
    94	  },
    95	  children: [/* content */]
    96	}]
    97	```
    98	
    99	**Common page sizes (DXA units, 1440 DXA = 1 inch):**
   100	
   101	| Paper | Width | Height | Content Width (1" margins) |
   102	|-------|-------|--------|---------------------------|
   103	| US Letter | 12,240 | 15,840 | 9,360 |
   104	| A4 (default) | 11,906 | 16,838 | 9,026 |
   105	
   106	**Landscape orientation:** docx-js swaps width/height internally, so pass portrait dimensions and let it handle the swap:
   107	```javascript
   108	size: {
   109	  width: 12240,   // Pass SHORT edge as width
   110	  height: 15840,  // Pass LONG edge as height
   111	  orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE  // docx-js swaps them in the XML
   112	},
   113	// Content width = 15840 - left margin - right margin (uses the long edge)
   114	```
   115	
   116	### Styles (Override Built-in Headings)
   117	
   118	Use Arial as the default font (universally supported). Keep titles black for readability.
   119	
   120	```javascript
   121	const doc = new Document({
   122	  styles: {
   123	    default: { document: { run: { font: "Arial", size: 24 } } }, // 12pt default
   124	    paragraphStyles: [
   125	      // IMPORTANT: Use exact IDs to override built-in styles
   126	      { id: "Heading1", name: "Heading 1", basedOn: "Normal", next: "Normal", quickFormat: true,
   127	        run: { size: 32, bold: true, font: "Arial" },
   128	        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 240, after: 240 }, outlineLevel: 0 } }, // outlineLevel required for TOC
   129	      { id: "Heading2", name: "Heading 2", basedOn: "Normal", next: "Normal", quickFormat: true,
   130	        run: { size: 28, bold: true, font: "Arial" },
   131	        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 180, after: 180 }, outlineLevel: 1 } },
   132	    ]
   133	  },
   134	  sections: [{
   135	    children: [
   136	      new Paragraph({ heading: HeadingLevel.HEADING_1, children: [new TextRun("Title")] }),
   137	    ]
   138	  }]
   139	});
   140	```
   141	
   142	### Lists (NEVER use unicode bullets)
   143	
   144	```javascript
   145	// ❌ WRONG - never manually insert bullet characters
   146	new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("• Item")] })  // BAD
   147	new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("\u2022 Item")] })  // BAD
   148	
   149	// ✅ CORRECT - use numbering config with LevelFormat.BULLET
   150	const doc = new Document({
   151	  numbering: {
   152	    config: [
   153	      { reference: "bullets",
   154	        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.BULLET, text: "•", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,
   155	          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },
   156	      { reference: "numbers",
   157	        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.DECIMAL, text: "%1.", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,
   158	          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },
   159	    ]
   160	  },
   161	  sections: [{
   162	    children: [
   163	      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: "bullets", level: 0 },
   164	        children: [new TextRun("Bullet item")] }),
   165	      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: "numbers", level: 0 },
   166	        children: [new TextRun("Numbered item")] }),
   167	    ]
   168	  }]
   169	});
   170	
   171	// ⚠️ Each reference creates INDEPENDENT numbering
   172	// Same reference = continues (1,2,3 then 4,5,6)
   173	// Different reference = restarts (1,2,3 then 1,2,3)
   174	```
   175	
   176	### Tables
   177	
   178	**CRITICAL: Tables need dual widths** - set both `columnWidths` on the table AND `width` on each cell. Without both, tables render incorrectly on some platforms.
   179	
   180	```javascript
   181	// CRITICAL: Always set table width for consistent rendering
   182	// CRITICAL: Use ShadingType.CLEAR (not SOLID) to prevent black backgrounds
   183	const border = { style: BorderStyle.SINGLE, size: 1, color: "CCCCCC" };
   184	const borders = { top: border, bottom: border, left: border, right: border };
   185	
   186	new Table({
   187	  width: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Always use DXA (percentages break in Google Docs)
   188	  columnWidths: [4680, 4680], // Must sum to table width (DXA: 1440 = 1 inch)
   189	  rows: [
   190	    new TableRow({
   191	      children: [
   192	        new TableCell({
   193	          borders,
   194	          width: { size: 4680, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Also set on each cell
   195	          shading: { fill: "D5E8F0", type: ShadingType.CLEAR }, // CLEAR not SOLID
   196	          margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }, // Cell padding (internal, not added to width)
   197	          children: [new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("Cell")] })]
   198	        })
   199	      ]
   200	    })
   201	  ]
   202	})
   203	```
   204	
   205	**Table width calculation:**
   206	
   207	Always use `WidthType.DXA` — `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` breaks in Google Docs.
   208	
   209	```javascript
   210	// Table width = sum of columnWidths = content width
   211	// US Letter with 1" margins: 12240 - 2880 = 9360 DXA
   212	width: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA },
   213	columnWidths: [7000, 2360]  // Must sum to table width
   214	```
   215	
   216	**Width rules:**
	< truncated lines 217-374 >
   375	}]
   376	```
   377	
   378	### Critical Rules for docx-js
   379	
   380	- **Set page size explicitly** - docx-js defaults to A4; use US Letter (12240 x 15840 DXA) for US documents
   381	- **Landscape: pass portrait dimensions** - docx-js swaps width/height internally; pass short edge as `width`, long edge as `height`, and set `orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE`
   382	- **Never use `\n`** - use separate Paragraph elements
   383	- **Never use unicode bullets** - use `LevelFormat.BULLET` with numbering config
   384	- **PageBreak must be in Paragraph** - standalone creates invalid XML
   385	- **ImageRun requires `type`** - always specify png/jpg/etc
   386	- **Always set table `width` with DXA** - never use `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` (breaks in Google Docs)
   387	- **Tables need dual widths** - `columnWidths` array AND cell `width`, both must match
   388	- **Table width = sum of columnWidths** - for DXA, ensure they add up exactly
   389	- **Always add cell margins** - use `margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }` for readable padding
   390	- **Use `ShadingType.CLEAR`** - never SOLID for table shading
   391	- **Never use tables as dividers/rules** - cells have minimum height and render as empty boxes (including in headers/footers); use `border: { bottom: { style: BorderStyle.SINGLE, size: 6, color: "2E75B6", space: 1 } }` on a Paragraph instead. For two-column footers, use tab stops (see Tab Stops section), not tables
   392	- **TOC requires HeadingLevel only** - no custom styles on heading paragraphs
   393	- **Override built-in styles** - use exact IDs: "Heading1", "Heading2", etc.
   394	- **Include `outlineLevel`** - required for TOC (0 for H1, 1 for H2, etc.)
   395	
   396	---
   397	
   398	## Editing Existing Documents
   399	
   400	**Follow all 3 steps in order.**
   401	
   402	### Step 1: Unpack
   403	```bash
   404	python scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/
   405	```
   406	Extracts XML, pretty-prints, merges adjacent runs, and converts smart quotes to XML entities (`“` etc.) so they survive editing. Use `--merge-runs false` to skip run merging.
   407	
   408	### Step 2: Edit XML
   409	
   410	Edit files in `unpacked/word/`. See XML Reference below for patterns.
   411	
   412	**Use "Claude" as the author** for tracked changes and comments, unless the user explicitly requests use of a different name.
   413	
   414	**Use the Edit tool directly for string replacement. Do not write Python scripts.** Scripts introduce unnecessary complexity. The Edit tool shows exactly what is being replaced.
   415	
   416	**CRITICAL: Use smart quotes for new content.** When adding text with apostrophes or quotes, use XML entities to produce smart quotes:
   417	```xml
   418	<!-- Use these entities for professional typography -->
   419	<w:t>Here’s a quote: “Hello”</w:t>
   420	```
   421	| Entity | Character |
   422	|--------|-----------|
   423	| `‘` | ‘ (left single) |
   424	| `’` | ’ (right single / apostrophe) |
   425	| `“` | “ (left double) |
   426	| `”` | ” (right double) |
   427	
   428	**Adding comments:** Use `comment.py` to handle boilerplate across multiple XML files (text must be pre-escaped XML):
   429	```bash
   430	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 "Comment text with & and ’"
   431	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 1 "Reply text" --parent 0  # reply to comment 0
   432	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 "Text" --author "Custom Author"  # custom author name
   433	```
   434	Then add markers to document.xml (see Comments in XML Reference).
   435	
   436	### Step 3: Pack
   437	```bash
   438	python scripts/office/pack.py unpacked/ output.docx --original document.docx
   439	```
   440	Validates with auto-repair, condenses XML, and creates DOCX. Use `--validate false` to skip.
   441	
   442	**Auto-repair will fix:**
   443	- `durableId` >= 0x7FFFFFFF (regenerates valid ID)
   444	- Missing `xml:space="preserve"` on `<w:t>` with whitespace
   445	
   446	**Auto-repair won't fix:**
   447	- Malformed XML, invalid element nesting, missing relationships, schema violations
   448	
   449	### Common Pitfalls
   450	
   451	- **Replace entire `<w:r>` elements**: When adding tracked changes, replace the whole `<w:r>...</w:r>` block with `<w:del>...<w:ins>...` as siblings. Don't inject tracked change tags inside a run.
   452	- **Preserve `<w:rPr>` formatting**: Copy the original run's `<w:rPr>` block into your tracked change runs to maintain bold, font size, etc.
   453	
   454	---
   455	
   456	## XML Reference
   457	
   458	### Schema Compliance
   459	
   460	- **Element order in `<w:pPr>`**: `<w:pStyle>`, `<w:numPr>`, `<w:spacing>`, `<w:ind>`, `<w:jc>`, `<w:rPr>` last
   461	- **Whitespace**: Add `xml:space="preserve"` to `<w:t>` with leading/trailing spaces
   462	- **RSIDs**: Must be 8-digit hex (e.g., `00AB1234`)
   463	
   464	### Tracked Changes
   465	
   466	**Insertion:**
   467	```xml
   468	<w:ins w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   469	  <w:r><w:t>inserted text</w:t></w:r>
   470	</w:ins>
   471	```
   472	
   473	**Deletion:**
   474	```xml
   475	<w:del w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   476	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>
   477	</w:del>
   478	```
   479	
   480	**Inside `<w:del>`**: Use `<w:delText>` instead of `<w:t>`, and `<w:delInstrText>` instead of `<w:instrText>`.
   481	
   482	**Minimal edits** - only mark what changes:
   483	```xml
   484	<!-- Change "30 days" to "60 days" -->
   485	<w:r><w:t>The term is </w:t></w:r>
   486	<w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="...">
   487	  <w:r><w:delText>30</w:delText></w:r>
   488	</w:del>
   489	<w:ins w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="...">
   490	  <w:r><w:t>60</w:t></w:r>
   491	</w:ins>
   492	<w:r><w:t> days.</w:t></w:r>
   493	```
   494	
   495	**Deleting entire paragraphs/list items** - when removing ALL content from a paragraph, also mark the paragraph mark as deleted so it merges with the next paragraph. Add `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`:
   496	```xml
   497	<w:p>
   498	  <w:pPr>
   499	    <w:numPr>...</w:numPr>  <!-- list numbering if present -->
   500	    <w:rPr>
   501	      <w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z"/>
   502	    </w:rPr>
   503	  </w:pPr>
   504	  <w:del w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   505	    <w:r><w:delText>Entire paragraph content being deleted...</w:delText></w:r>
   506	  </w:del>
   507	</w:p>
   508	```
   509	Without the `<w:del/>` in `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`, accepting changes leaves an empty paragraph/list item.
   510	
   511	**Rejecting another author's insertion** - nest deletion inside their insertion:
   512	```xml
   513	<w:ins w:author="Jane" w:id="5">
   514	  <w:del w:author="Claude" w:id="10">
   515	    <w:r><w:delText>their inserted text</w:delText></w:r>
   516	  </w:del>
   517	</w:ins>
   518	```
   519	
   520	**Restoring another author's deletion** - add insertion after (don't modify their deletion):
   521	```xml
   522	<w:del w:author="Jane" w:id="5">
   523	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>
   524	</w:del>
   525	<w:ins w:author="Claude" w:id="10">
   526	  <w:r><w:t>deleted text</w:t></w:r>
   527	</w:ins>
   528	```
   529	
   530	### Comments
   531	
   532	After running `comment.py` (see Step 2), add markers to document.xml. For replies, use `--parent` flag and nest markers inside the parent's.
   533	
   534	**CRITICAL: `<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` are siblings of `<w:r>`, never inside `<w:r>`.**
   535	
   536	```xml
   537	<!-- Comment markers are direct children of w:p, never inside w:r -->
   538	<w:commentRangeStart w:id="0"/>
   539	<w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   540	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted</w:delText></w:r>
   541	</w:del>
   542	<w:r><w:t> more text</w:t></w:r>
   543	<w:commentRangeEnd w:id="0"/>
   544	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="0"/></w:r>
   545	
   546	<!-- Comment 0 with reply 1 nested inside -->
   547	<w:commentRangeStart w:id="0"/>
   548	  <w:commentRangeStart w:id="1"/>
   549	  <w:r><w:t>text</w:t></w:r>
   550	  <w:commentRangeEnd w:id="1"/>
   551	<w:commentRangeEnd w:id="0"/>
   552	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="0"/></w:r>
   553	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="1"/></w:r>
   554	```
   555	
   556	### Images
   557	
   558	1. Add image file to `word/media/`
   559	2. Add relationship to `word/_rels/document.xml.rels`:
   560	```xml
   561	<Relationship Id="rId5" Type=".../image" Target="media/image1.png"/>
   562	```
   563	3. Add content type to `[Content_Types].xml`:
   564	```xml
   565	<Default Extension="png" ContentType="image/png"/>
   566	```
   567	4. Reference in document.xml:
   568	```xml
   569	<w:drawing>
   570	  <wp:inline>
   571	    <wp:extent cx="914400" cy="914400"/>  <!-- EMUs: 914400 = 1 inch -->
   572	    <a:graphic>
   573	      <a:graphicData uri=".../picture">
   574	        <pic:pic>
   575	          <pic:blipFill><a:blip r:embed="rId5"/></pic:blipFill>
   576	        </pic:pic>
   577	      </a:graphicData>
   578	    </a:graphic>
   579	  </wp:inline>
   580	</w:drawing>
   581	```
   582	
   583	---
   584	
   585	## Dependencies
   586	
   587	- **pandoc**: Text extraction
   588	- **docx**: `npm install -g docx` (new documents)
   589	- **LibreOffice**: PDF conversion (auto-configured for sandboxed environments via `scripts/office/soffice.py`)
   590	- **Poppler**: `pdftoppm` for images
   591

content.text:
217	- **Always use `WidthType.DXA`** — never `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` (incompatible with Google Docs)
   218	- Table width must equal the sum of `columnWidths`
   219	- Cell `width` must match corresponding `columnWidth`
   220	- Cell `margins` are internal padding - they reduce content area, not add to cell width
   221	- For full-width tables: use content width (page width minus left and right margins)
   222	
   223	### Images
   224	
   225	```javascript
   226	// CRITICAL: type parameter is REQUIRED
   227	new Paragraph({
   228	  children: [new ImageRun({
   229	    type: "png", // Required: png, jpg, jpeg, gif, bmp, svg
   230	    data: fs.readFileSync("image.png"),
   231	    transformation: { width: 200, height: 150 },
   232	    altText: { title: "Title", description: "Desc", name: "Name" } // All three required
   233	  })]
   234	})
   235	```
   236	
   237	### Page Breaks
   238	
   239	```javascript
   240	// CRITICAL: PageBreak must be inside a Paragraph
   241	new Paragraph({ children: [new PageBreak()] })
   242	
   243	// Or use pageBreakBefore
   244	new Paragraph({ pageBreakBefore: true, children: [new TextRun("New page")] })
   245	```
   246	
   247	### Hyperlinks
   248	
   249	```javascript
   250	// External link
   251	new Paragraph({
   252	  children: [new ExternalHyperlink({
   253	    children: [new TextRun({ text: "Click here", style: "Hyperlink" })],
   254	    link: "https://example.com",
   255	  })]
   256	})
   257	
   258	// Internal link (bookmark + reference)
   259	// 1. Create bookmark at destination
   260	new Paragraph({ heading: HeadingLevel.HEADING_1, children: [
   261	  new Bookmark({ id: "chapter1", children: [new TextRun("Chapter 1")] }),
   262	]})
   263	// 2. Link to it
   264	new Paragraph({ children: [new InternalHyperlink({
   265	  children: [new TextRun({ text: "See Chapter 1", style: "Hyperlink" })],
   266	  anchor: "chapter1",
   267	})]})
   268	```
   269	
   270	### Footnotes
   271	
   272	```javascript
   273	const doc = new Document({
   274	  footnotes: {
   275	    1: { children: [new Paragraph("Source: Annual Report 2024")] },
   276	    2: { children: [new Paragraph("See appendix for methodology")] },
   277	  },
   278	  sections: [{
   279	    children: [new Paragraph({
   280	      children: [
   281	        new TextRun("Revenue grew 15%"),
   282	        new FootnoteReferenceRun(1),
   283	        new TextRun(" using adjusted metrics"),
   284	        new FootnoteReferenceRun(2),
   285	      ],
   286	    })]
   287	  }]
   288	});
   289	```
   290	
   291	### Tab Stops
   292	
   293	```javascript
   294	// Right-align text on same line (e.g., date opposite a title)
   295	new Paragraph({
   296	  children: [
   297	    new TextRun("Company Name"),
   298	    new TextRun("\tJanuary 2025"),
   299	  ],
   300	  tabStops: [{ type: TabStopType.RIGHT, position: TabStopPosition.MAX }],
   301	})
   302	
   303	// Dot leader (e.g., TOC-style)
   304	new Paragraph({
   305	  children: [
   306	    new TextRun("Introduction"),
   307	    new TextRun({ children: [
   308	      new PositionalTab({
   309	        alignment: PositionalTabAlignment.RIGHT,
   310	        relativeTo: PositionalTabRelativeTo.MARGIN,
   311	        leader: PositionalTabLeader.DOT,
   312	      }),
   313	      "3",
   314	    ]}),
   315	  ],
   316	})
   317	```
   318	
   319	### Multi-Column Layouts
   320	
   321	```javascript
   322	// Equal-width columns
   323	sections: [{
   324	  properties: {
   325	    column: {
   326	      count: 2,          // number of columns
   327	      space: 720,        // gap between columns in DXA (720 = 0.5 inch)
   328	      equalWidth: true,
   329	      separate: true,    // vertical line between columns
   330	    },
   331	  },
   332	  children: [/* content flows naturally across columns */]
   333	}]
   334	
   335	// Custom-width columns (equalWidth must be false)
   336	sections: [{
   337	  properties: {
   338	    column: {
   339	      equalWidth: false,
   340	      children: [
   341	        new Column({ width: 5400, space: 720 }),
   342	        new Column({ width: 3240 }),
   343	      ],
   344	    },
   345	  },
   346	  children: [/* content */]
   347	}]
   348	```
   349	
   350	Force a column break with a new section using `type: SectionType.NEXT_COLUMN`.
   351	
   352	### Table of Contents
   353	
   354	```javascript
   355	// CRITICAL: Headings must use HeadingLevel ONLY - no custom styles
   356	new TableOfContents("Table of Contents", { hyperlink: true, headingStyleRange: "1-3" })
   357	```
   358	
   359	### Headers/Footers
   360	
   361	```javascript
   362	sections: [{
   363	  properties: {

   365	  },

   371	      children: [new TextRun("Page "), new TextRun({ children: [PageNumber.CURRENT] })]

name: Creating reusable skills in Claude
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The user inquired about creating custom commands in Claude, specifically wanting an "approve" command that would automatically update a progress.md file and push changes to git. They were particularly interested in whether this functionality existed within Claude Code projects. Claude explained that there are multiple approaches available, with the most relevant being Claude Code's custom slash command system. The conversation progressed from discussing Claude.ai Projects with custom instructions to focusing on Claude Code's `.claude/commands/` directory structure, which allows users to create true slash commands by placing markdown files containing instructions in either the project-specific `.claude/commands/` folder or the user's home directory for global commands. The user specifically asked how to implement this as an actual slash command, leading to clarification that Claude Code supports official custom slash commands through markdown files, where a file like `approve.md` containing the desired workflow instructions can be invoked with `/approve`. This approach provides the persistent, project-scoped automation the user was seeking, allowing them to standardize their git workflow with a simple command.

chat_messages.text: in claude can i create a skill that lets for example always instructs to update progress.md and push to git. Like a aprrove command

content.text: in claude can i create a skill that lets for example always instructs to update progress.md and push to git. Like a aprrove command

chat_messages.text:
Yes! You can create something like this using **Projects** in Claude.ai. Here's how:

## Using a Project with Custom Instructions

Create a Project and add instructions like:

> Whenever I type `/approve`, update `progress.md` with what we accomplished and provide the git commands to commit and push.

This effectively gives you a slash-command-style workflow within that project.

---

## Your Options

**1. Project System Prompt (best for this)**
In a Claude Project, set instructions that define your custom commands. Example:

> **Commands I use:**
> - `/approve` — Summarize what we built, update `progress.md` with a new entry (date + summary), then output the git commands: `git add progress.md && git commit -m "..." && git push`

**2. Reusable Prompt (manual)**
Save a prompt snippet you paste in when needed. Less elegant, but works outside Projects.

**3. Claude Code (most powerful)**
If you use **Claude Code** (the CLI tool), you can create a `CLAUDE.md` file in your repo that acts as persistent instructions. This is essentially the "skill" system you're describing — you define behaviors, conventions, and commands that Claude always follows in that project context.

In `CLAUDE.md` you'd write:
```markdown
## Commands
- When the user says "approve": update progress.md with today's date and a summary of what was done, then run `git add progress.md && git commit -m "<summary>" && git push`
```

Claude Code reads this file automatically on every session.

---

**Claude Code + `CLAUDE.md` is probably closest to what you're envisioning** — it's persistent, project-scoped, and Claude actually executes the git commands rather than just suggesting them. Would you like help setting that up?

chat_messages.text: am i ment in my project with claude code

content.text: am i ment in my project with claude code

chat_messages.text:
Yes! In Claude Code, the `CLAUDE.md` file is exactly the "skill" system you're thinking of. It's designed for this.

In your project root, create a `CLAUDE.md` with something like:

```markdown
## Commands

### /approve
When I type `/approve`:
1. Summarize what we just built/changed
2. Update `progress.md` with a new entry: date, summary, what files changed
3. Run: `git add . && git commit -m "<summary>" && git push`
```

Then whenever you type `/approve` in Claude Code, it will follow those instructions automatically every session in that project.

You can add as many custom commands/behaviors as you want in there — coding conventions, workflow rules, shortcuts, etc. It's essentially your persistent instruction layer for that codebase.

```markdown
## Commands

### /approve
When I type `/approve`:

2. Update `progress.md` with a new entry: date, summary, what files changed
3. Run: `git add . && git commit -m "<summary>" && git push`
```

Then whenever you type `/approve` in Claude Code, it will follow those instructions automatically every session in that project.

You can add as many custom commands/behaviors as you want in there — coding conventions, workflow rules, shortcuts, etc. It's essentially your persistent instruction layer for that codebase.

chat_messages.text: how to make it a slash command?

content.text: how to make it a slash command?

chat_messages.text:
Claude Code doesn't have true built-in slash commands from `CLAUDE.md` — but there are a couple of ways to get close:

## Option 1: Custom Slash Commands (Official Feature)
Claude Code supports custom slash commands via markdown files in `.claude/commands/`:

```
your-project/
└── .claude/
    └── commands/
        └── approve.md
```

Inside `approve.md`:
```markdown
Summarize what we just built, update progress.md with today's date and a summary, then run: git add . && git commit -m "<summary>" && git push
```

Then in Claude Code you can type `/approve` and it runs that instruction. ✅

---

## Option 2: Project-wide vs Personal Commands

- `.claude/commands/` in your **repo** → shared with your team
- `~/.claude/commands/` in your **home dir** → personal commands available in all projects

---

This is the cleanest approach. The `/approve` command becomes a real slash command you can invoke, and the markdown file is easy to edit and version control alongside your code.

## Option 2: Project-wide vs Personal Commands

---

name: Excel file analysis capabilities
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person requested a comprehensive data integration and quality analysis task involving two Excel files: a current database file ("Active_StationsRoads_3-9-2026_9-12-45_AM.xlsx") and a new data file ("Keliai_03_06.xlsx"). The goal was to update the existing database with new information without deleting any existing records, while also identifying data quality issues in the new file.

Claude performed the full analysis programmatically using Python (pandas, openpyxl) and delivered a four-sheet Excel report ("Data_Integration_Report.xlsx"). The key matching field between the two datasets was RoadID, which required numeric normalization (the current file stored RoadIDs as strings while the new file used floats). After normalization, 2,136 records in the new file matched existing RoadIDs (updates), and 309 records were entirely new. Bad data flagging identified 308 total problematic records across three categories: 13 records with missing RoadID, 294 duplicate RoadID + StationOpkisCode combinations, and 1 record with an invalid prWay value ("Not found"). The cleaned integration table contained 2,150 records ready for import. The primary field-level changes in the update set were population of the previously empty RCP_Station_ID_(PLC) field and normalization of StationOpkisCode from float to integer format. The final report included a summary dashboard with KPI tiles, a color-coded bad data sheet, a field-level changes detail sheet, and the cleaned integration table.
